short_definition: Current or reference system based on drained peat/organic soils used for arable crops, intensive grassland, dairy, forage or biogas-related production.
typical_current_land_use: arable crops; intensive grassland; dairy/forage systems; biogas feedstock
methane_risk_note: Typically low CH4 risk under drainage, but high CO2 emissions from peat oxidation.
main_benefit: Maintains existing production, machinery, markets and farm routines.
main_tradeoff: High GHG emissions from drained organic soils and long-term peat degradation/subsidence.
key_uncertainty: Magnitude of emissions varies by soil, drainage depth, land use and water-table depth.
supporting_papers: Freeman et al. 2022|Girkin et al. 2023|Lloyd et al. 2023|Heuts et al. 2024
visual_encoding_hint: Left-most dark/brown node; high emissions, high agricultural continuity.
atlas_card_title: The status quo: productive, but emission-intensive
atlas_card_text: Drainage-based agriculture maintains current production systems but is the reference state against which rewetting and transition pathways are assessed.

short_definition: Technical or management-oriented adjustment of drainage and groundwater levels without necessarily changing the dominant land-use system.
typical_current_land_use: grassland; dairy; forage production; arable peat soils where controlled drainage is feasible
water_table_requirement: moderately raised water table; controlled or shallow drainage
methane_risk_note: CH4 risk usually lower than under full rewetting, but CO2 reduction may also be limited if water table remains too low.
main_benefit: Lower disruption for farms and potentially easier first step than full land-use transformation.
main_tradeoff: May deliver limited GHG/ecological benefits if not accompanied by meaningful water-table rise or reduced land-use intensity.
key_uncertainty: Effectiveness depends on hydrological feasibility, infrastructure, water availability and actual water-table response.
supporting_papers: Purola & Lehtonen 2022|Niemi et al. 2024|Heuts et al. 2024|Wils et al. 2025
visual_encoding_hint: Intermediate node; high compatibility but uncertain mitigation.
atlas_card_text: Partial water-table management can be less disruptive, but the literature warns that shallow drainage without real land-use change may underperform.

short_definition: Grassland or pasture maintained under elevated groundwater conditions, aiming to reduce peat oxidation while retaining forage production.
water_table_requirement: raised groundwater, often around moderately high but still trafficable conditions
methane_risk_note: Potential CH4 trade-offs if water table approaches the surface; site-specific monitoring needed.
main_benefit: Maintains recognisable grassland production and fits dairy/forage regions better than many specialised paludiculture crops.
main_tradeoff: Reduced load-bearing capacity, seasonality and machinery access can constrain management.
key_uncertainty: Yield, forage quality and trafficability depend strongly on water table, soil moisture, season and farm machinery.
supporting_papers: van Dijk et al. 2026|Jongbloed et al. 2026|Liu et al. 2023|Aben et al. 2024
visual_encoding_hint: Large highlighted node in South Germany Fit view.
atlas_card_text: Raised-water-table pasture is one of the most plausible pathways for dairy and forage regions, but trafficability becomes a key constraint.

short_definition: Mowing of wet or partially rewetted grasslands for hay, litter, biomass, compost, biogas feedstock or low-intensity forage uses.
typical_current_land_use: grassland; wet meadow; low-intensity forage or biomass use
water_table_requirement: high but usually not permanently surface-flooded; harvest windows required
methane_risk_note: CH4 risk depends on water-table dynamics, vegetation and inundation duration.
main_benefit: Agriculturally recognisable and potentially compatible with existing meadow/forage/biomass traditions.
main_tradeoff: Biomass quality, harvest timing and machinery access are uncertain under high water levels.
key_uncertainty: Whether wet biomass can be integrated into existing dairy, forage or biogas systems without undermining GHG benefits.
supporting_papers: Liu et al. 2023|Temmink et al. 2026|Merkelbach et al. 2026|Hünnebeck-Wells et al. 2025
visual_encoding_hint: Bridge pathway between peat pasture and specialised biomass crops.
atlas_card_title: The wet meadow as transition space
atlas_card_text: Wet mowing may be easier to recognise as farming than full restoration, but it needs viable harvest logistics and biomass outlets.

pathway_name: Reed canary grass biomass / Phalaris paludiculture
short_definition: Production of reed canary grass biomass on wet or rewetted peat soils for biomass, fodder, energy or material-oriented uses.
typical_current_land_use: converted cropland/grassland on peat; wet biomass fields
water_table_requirement: high water table; peat-preserving hydrology required
methane_risk_note: Potential CH4 and N2O trade-offs depend on site heterogeneity, nutrient status and management intensity.
main_benefit: More agriculturally familiar than reed/Typha and can provide a biomass pathway under wetter conditions.
main_tradeoff: GHG performance is strongly site-dependent and market/value-chain development may be limited.
key_uncertainty: Field heterogeneity, nutrient status, harvest intensity and actual achieved water table.
supporting_papers: Thers et al. 2023|Nielsen et al. 2024|Rodriguez et al. 2025|Temmink et al. 2026
atlas_card_text: Reed canary grass can reduce emissions compared with drained annual crops, but results depend on hydrology, nutrients and harvest management.

short_definition: Use of common reed biomass for thatching, construction, insulation, energy, compost or water-treatment related pathways.
typical_current_land_use: reed-dominated wetlands; wet biomass areas; coastal/wetland management sites
water_table_requirement: high to very high; reed-dominated wetland conditions
methane_risk_note: Very wet conditions may increase CH4 risk; net climate effect depends on site and product substitution.
main_benefit: Concrete value-chain examples show that wet biomass can become a product, not only a conservation by-product.
main_tradeoff: Requires specialised harvesting, processing infrastructure, quality standards and market access.
key_uncertainty: Regional demand and processing capacity, especially outside traditional thatching regions.
supporting_papers: Becker et al. 2020|Köbbing et al. 2013|Čížková et al. 2023|Ziegler et al. 2021
visual_encoding_hint: Specialised product pathway node with high market dependency.
atlas_card_title: From wet biomass to product chains
atlas_card_text: Reed illustrates that paludiculture needs not only biomass, but also harvest logistics, processing and markets.

short_definition: Use of robust livestock such as water buffalo for wet grassland, reed regulation, habitat management and low-intensity grazing.
typical_current_land_use: wet grassland; reed-dominated sites; conservation grazing; mosaic landscapes
water_table_requirement: wet to very wet; difficult-to-machine sites can be suitable
methane_risk_note: Animal CH4, dung/urine and trampling must be considered; not automatically climate-positive.
main_benefit: Can keep wet landscapes open and manage reed/invasive species where machinery access is limited.
main_tradeoff: Niche system with animal-emission, market, management and welfare considerations.
key_uncertainty: Stocking density, vegetation response, GHG balance and viable regional marketing.
supporting_papers: Sweers et al. 2013|Fűrész et al. 2023|Merkelbach et al. 2026|Hansson et al. 2023
visual_encoding_hint: Small specialised node; high context dependency.
atlas_card_title: A niche for wet, rough ground
atlas_card_text: Water buffalo grazing can be useful for wet and reed-dominated sites, but it is a specialised management option, not a universal substitute.

short_definition: Restoration or conservation trajectory with full or near-full withdrawal from conventional agricultural production.
typical_current_land_use: restoration areas; conservation peatlands; abandoned or converted agricultural peatlands
water_table_requirement: near-surface water table or restored peatland hydrology
methane_risk_note: Short- to medium-term CH4 increases may occur depending on vegetation, water level and site history.
main_benefit: Strong peat-preservation and biodiversity potential where hydrology can be restored.
main_tradeoff: Loss of agricultural production and high need for compensation, land management and governance.
key_uncertainty: Time horizon of net GHG balance, stakeholder acceptance, water availability and land ownership.
supporting_papers: Girkin et al. 2023|Freeman et al. 2022|Niemi et al. 2024|Nordbeck et al. 2025
visual_encoding_hint: Right-most blue/green node; high mitigation but low agricultural continuity.
atlas_card_title: The restoration end of the spectrum
atlas_card_text: Restoration can maximise peatland functions, but in productive agricultural regions it raises major compensation, land-use and governance questions.
